title: Do not hardcode secrets or long-lived credentials
text: Hardcoded tokens, passwords, API keys, and similar credentials create rotation, exposure, and audit problems. Prefer runtime-provided secrets with narrowly scoped access.
summary: Secret-like literals should live in secret management or deployment configuration, not in source.
embedding_text:
rule_no: SEC.1
pack_id: shared_security
language_id: shared
context_id: 
dialect_id: 
section: SEC
title: Do not hardcode secrets or long-lived credentials
summary: Secret-like literals should live in secret management or deployment configuration, not in source.
keywords: not, secrets, credentials, sec.1, hardcode, long-lived, secret-like, literals, should, live, secret, management
category: security
reviewability: auto_review
trigger_patterns: hardcoded_secret
fix_guidance: Move the secret into environment-backed configuration or a secret manager and inject it at runtime.
text: Hardcoded tokens, passwords, API keys, and similar credentials create rotation, exposure, and audit problems. Prefer runtime-provided secrets with narrowly scoped access.
fix_guidance: Move the secret into environment-backed configuration or a secret manager and inject it at runtime.

title: Build shell commands from trusted primitives, not concatenated strings
text: Command construction through string concatenation is fragile and exposes injection risk. Prefer argument vectors, explicit allow-lists, or dedicated APIs.
summary: Shell execution paths should not interpolate untrusted fragments into a command string.
embedding_text:

language_id: shared
context_id:

title: Build shell commands from trusted primitives, not concatenated strings

keywords: shell, not, command, sec.2, build, commands, trusted, primitives, concatenated, strings, execution, paths
category: security
reviewability: auto_review
trigger_patterns: shell_injection
fix_guidance: Use structured subprocess or exec APIs that separate the executable from validated arguments.
text: Command construction through string concatenation is fragile and exposes injection risk. Prefer argument vectors, explicit allow-lists, or dedicated APIs.
fix_guidance: Use structured subprocess or exec APIs that separate the executable from validated arguments.

title: Parameterize SQL instead of composing query text dynamically
text: SQL should be parameterized through the database driver's bind facilities or a higher-level abstraction. Dynamic text assembly should be reserved for carefully controlled structural clauses.
summary: Query text built from variables is difficult to reason about and easy to misuse.
embedding_text:

category: security
reviewability: auto_review
trigger_patterns: sql_injection, dynamic_sql
fix_guidance: Bind values separately from the SQL string and constrain structural query changes through allow-listed fragments.
text: SQL should be parameterized through the database driver's bind facilities or a higher-level abstraction. Dynamic text assembly should be reserved for carefully controlled structural clauses.
fix_guidance: Bind values separately from the SQL string and constrain structural query changes through allow-listed fragments.

title: Do not leave fallback secrets or test credentials in production code paths
text: Even when intended only for local development or bootstrap, embedded fallback secrets create a second and weaker trust path that is easy to forget during incident response or key rotation.
summary: Backup credentials often become the least-audited credential in the system.

category: security
reviewability: auto_review
trigger_patterns: hardcoded_secret
fix_guidance: Remove embedded fallback credentials and require runtime configuration or a dedicated local-only development path instead.
text: Even when intended only for local development or bootstrap, embedded fallback secrets create a second and weaker trust path that is easy to forget during incident response or key rotation.
fix_guidance: Remove embedded fallback credentials and require runtime configuration or a dedicated local-only development path instead.

title: Keep command execution APIs structured so the executable and arguments remain distinct
text: Separating the executable from validated arguments narrows the injection surface and makes the trust boundary visible in code review across languages and runtimes.
summary: Security review is much stronger when command construction never depends on ad hoc shell parsing.

category: security
reviewability: auto_review

fix_guidance: Use structured process APIs, validate each argument according to its contract, and avoid passing shell fragments through string concatenation.
text: Separating the executable from validated arguments narrows the injection surface and makes the trust boundary visible in code review across languages and runtimes.

title: Dynamic query structure should stay allow-listed even when values are parameterized
text: ORDER BY fragments, table identifiers, and optional clauses can still create injection or correctness risk when the allowed structures are not tightly bounded.
summary: Parameter binding protects values, not uncontrolled structural SQL changes.

fix_guidance: Keep structural variation behind a small allow-list and continue binding values separately from the SQL text.

title: Example, bootstrap, and fallback credentials in committed code should be treated as real secrets unless clearly impossible to activate
text: Backup and bootstrap secrets often become the least-audited authentication path in a system because everyone assumes they are temporary or unreachable.
summary: "Test" or "dev" labels do not remove rotation, misuse, or confusion risk once credentials are committed.

reviewability: auto_review

fix_guidance: Remove committed fallback credentials or move them into an isolated local-only development path that cannot ship as a production default.

title: Avoid unsafe any escape hatches at API boundaries
text: Using any in boundary types removes the compiler's ability to defend interface contracts and makes downstream code rely on convention instead of proof.
summary: any erases the checker exactly where it is most useful for long-term maintenance.

category: type_usage
reviewability: auto_review
trigger_patterns: any_type
fix_guidance: Replace any with a narrower structural type, unknown plus validation, or a dedicated domain type.
text: Using any in boundary types removes the compiler's ability to defend interface contracts and makes downstream code rely on convention instead of proof.
fix_guidance: Replace any with a narrower structural type, unknown plus validation, or a dedicated domain type.

title: Treat non-null assertions as proof obligations
text: The non-null assertion operator moves correctness from the checker to human reasoning. Prefer explicit guards or type narrowing when values may actually be absent.
summary: A non-null assertion should be a last resort, not the main way to silence control-flow uncertainty.

category: type_usage
reviewability: auto_review
trigger_patterns: non_null_assertion
fix_guidance: Add a guard, early return, or validation step so the type checker can prove the value is present.
text: The non-null assertion operator moves correctness from the checker to human reasoning. Prefer explicit guards or type narrowing when values may actually be absent.
fix_guidance: Add a guard, early return, or validation step so the type checker can prove the value is present.

title: any should not cross public or persistence boundaries without validation
text: Boundary code benefits most from TypeScript proof. Using any there makes downstream code depend on convention and defensive casting instead of a checked contract.
summary: The most expensive place to lose types is exactly where data enters or leaves the system.

fix_guidance: Replace any with unknown plus validation, a narrower interface, or a domain type that communicates the boundary contract.
text: Boundary code benefits most from TypeScript proof. Using any there makes downstream code depend on convention and defensive casting instead of a checked contract.

title: Non-null assertions should follow a local guard or invariant the reader can verify
text: A non-null assertion is easier to trust when the code has just checked the value, constructed it locally, or otherwise established the invariant nearby.
summary: Silencing the checker is acceptable only when the proof is visible in the same small region.

title: Async boundaries should preserve rejection handling and caller ownership
text: TypeScript async code stays maintainable when the call site makes it explicit whether work is fire-and-forget, joined, or propagated to the next layer.
summary: Promise work that is neither awaited nor returned is difficult to reason about and easy to lose.

reviewability: auto_review
trigger_patterns: promise_without_await
fix_guidance: Return or await the promise, or document the intentionally detached background behavior in a dedicated helper.
text: TypeScript async code stays maintainable when the call site makes it explicit whether work is fire-and-forget, joined, or propagated to the next layer.

title: Do not rely on @ts-ignore as a steady-state compatibility mechanism
text: A long-lived @ts-ignore hides real drift between the code and its types. It should be temporary, localized, and justified by a tracked limitation.
summary: Type suppression comments tend to outlive the one-off migration they were introduced for.

title: Validate untrusted JSON at runtime before trusting its shape
text: JSON.parse returns unknown runtime data even when the code immediately casts it. The shape should be validated at the boundary before the rest of the system assumes a richer type.
summary: Parsed JSON is data, not proof of a TypeScript contract.

text: Type suppression is easiest to manage when the unsafe boundary is localized, commented, and tracked as a compatibility exception instead of a normal coding pattern.

text: Application boundaries are stronger when parsing and validation happen together, so the rest of the codebase can rely on a true checked type instead of unchecked assumptions.

title: Avoid cast chains that combine any and parsed data at untrusted boundaries
text: A boundary that receives external data should not immediately cast it through any into a rich application type without validation in between.

title: Treat @ts-expect-error as temporary debt with a local explanation and blast-radius control
text: `@ts-expect-error` should live next to a known limitation and should not become a blanket compatibility mechanism for ordinary code evolution.
summary: Expect-error is safer than silent suppression only when the incompatible boundary remains explicit and short-lived.

title: Double casts through unknown should be treated as missing validation until proven otherwise
text: A double cast can sometimes bridge library type gaps, but at untrusted boundaries it usually means the code is skipping the validation step that should justify the domain type.
summary: `as unknown as T` often indicates that runtime data is being trusted without a real proof step.
